://>://>://>://>Hi, I'm not sure if I understand your question correctly, but I'll try to answer it as best I can. The planning phase in Kanban is done in a different way than in Scrum. In Kanban, the planning is done in a continuous manner, rather than in a timeboxed sprint. This means that work is pulled from the backlog as capacity allows, rather than being committed to a specific sprint. The team is responsible for prioritizing the work and deciding what to work on next, and the work is pulled from the backlog as capacity allows. This allows for more flexibility and adaptability, as the team can respond to changing priorities and customer needs. However, this also means that there is no fixed timebox for the work, and the team may not have a clear idea of when a feature will be completed. In Scrum, on the other hand, the planning is done in a more structured way, with a timeboxed sprint. The team commits to a specific amount of work for the sprint, and the work is planned and estimated in detail. This allows for a more predictable and focused approach, as the team knows exactly what they need to do and when they need to do it. However, this also means that the team may not be able to respond to changing priorities or customer needs as quickly as they could with Kanban. So, it really depends on the specific needs and goals of the team and the project. If you want more flexibility and adaptability, Kanban may be a better fit. If you want more predictability and focus, Scrum may be a better fit. It's also worth noting that both frameworks can be used together, with Kanban being used to visualize the workflow and Scrum being used for planning and timeboxing. This can be a good way to get the best of both worlds. I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.://>://>://>://>://>://>://>://>://

you want to filter out only those that match a specific domain, you can use a regular expression to do so. A regular expression is a pattern that describes a set of strings, and it can be used to match and extract specific parts of a string.

In this case, we want to match email addresses that end with @johnpaulprep.org. We can use the following regular expression to do so:

/^.*@johnpaulprep\.org$/

This regular expression matches any string that starts with any number of characters, followed by an @ symbol, followed by the domain name johnpaulprep.org, followed by any number of characters. The $ symbol at the end of the expression indicates that the match must end at the end of the string.

To use this regular expression in Python, you can use the re module. Here's an example of how you can use it to filter email addresses:

import re

email_addresses = ['john@example.com', 'jane@johnpaulprep.org', 'jim@johndoe.com']

filtered_email_addresses = []

for email_address in email_addresses:
if re.match(r'^.*@johnpaulprep\.org$', email_address):
filtered_email_addresses.append(email_address)

print(filtered_email_addresses)

In this example, we first import the re module. Then, we create a list of email addresses. We then use a for loop to iterate over each email address in the list, and use the re.match() method to check if the email address matches the regular expression. If it does, we add it to a new list called filtered_email_addresses. Finally, we print the filtered list of email addresses.

from bokeh.plotting import figure, output_file, show
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, HoverTool
from bokeh.embed import components

# Create a figure
p = figure(x_axis_type="datetime", width=800, height=250)
p.line(x="date", y="close", source=source, line_width=2)

# Add a hover tool
hover = HoverTool(tooltips=[("Date", "@date{%F}"), ("Close", "@close")])
p.add_tools(hover)

# Create a ColumnDataSource
source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(date=dates, close=prices))

# Add a title and axis labels
p.title.text = "Stock Prices"
p.xaxis.axis_label = "Date"
p.yaxis.axis_label = "Close Price"

# Render the plot
output_file("stock_prices.html")
show(p)
```
This code creates a Bokeh figure with a line chart, adds a hover tool, and creates a ColumnDataSource with the stock prices data. 30fps or 60fps?
When capturing video from a VHS tape using OBS, the frame rate you select will depend on the specific requirements of your project and the type of video you are working with.

The frame rate is the number of frames per second that the video is captured at. The higher the frame rate, the smoother the video will appear, but it will also require more storage space and may result in a larger file size.

If you are working with a VHS tape that has a lower resolution, such as 480p or 720p, then it's recommended to capture the video at a lower frame rate, such as 30fps. This will help to reduce the file size and make it easier to work with the video in post-production.

If you are working with a VHS tape that has a higher resolution, such as 1080p, then it's recommended to capture the video at a higher frame rate, such as 60fps. This will help to capture the video at a higher quality and make it easier to work with the video in post-production.

In summary, when capturing video from a VHS tape using OBS, the frame rate you select will depend on the specific requirements of your project and the type of video you are working with. If you are working with a lower resolution VHS tape, then it's recommended to capture the video at a lower frame rate, such as 30fps. If you are working with a higher resolution VHS tape, then it's recommended to capture the video at a higher frame rate, such as 60fps.

://> Yes, that's correct. The terms "stash" and "repository" are often used in the context of software development, but they have different meanings and connotations.

A "stash" is typically used to refer to a temporary storage area for changes to a project that have not yet been committed to the main codebase. A stash is usually local to the developer's machine, and is not shared with other members of the development team. Stashes are often used when a developer needs to switch to working on a different branch or task, but doesn't want to commit their changes yet.

On the other hand, a "repository" is a central location where the complete history of a project is stored, along with all the files, code, and other assets that make up the project. A repository is usually shared among all members of a development team, who can access, modify, and collaborate on the project files. A repository typically includes version control tools that allow developers to track changes to the project over time, and to collaborate on the same codebase.

In summary, a stash is a temporary storage area for changes that haven't been committed to the main codebase, while a repository is a central location for the entire project, including all its history

Mediawiki uses a combination of the `User` table and the `User_groups` table to store user information, including passwords. The `User` table stores basic user information such as the username, email address, and password hash. The `User_groups` table stores the user's group memberships, which can be used to control access to different parts of the wiki.

The password hash is stored in the `User` table using the `user_password` field. The password hash is generated using the `password_hash()` function in PHP, which uses the `PASSWORD_BCRYPT` algorithm by default. This algorithm is a one-way hash function that is designed to be slow and memory-intensive, making it difficult for attackers to crack the password.

To set up a new Mediawiki installation, you will need to create a new database and import the Mediawiki database schema. You can then use the `Special:UserLogin` page to create new users and set their passwords. Mediawiki will automatically generate a password hash for each user and store it in the `User` table. You do not need to set up your own SQL database for the password hashes, as Mediawiki will handle this for you.

so that the output is a new excel file with a tab for each unique combination of the "CPT" and "BlueHost" columns?
Yes, you can do that by using the `groupby()` function and the `size()` function of the dataframe. Here's an example:
```
import pandas as pd

#Read in the csv file
df = pd.read_csv('your_file.csv')

#Create a new dataframe containing each unique combination of the "CPT" column and the "BlueHost" column
df_grouped = df.groupby(['CPT','BlueHost']).size().reset_index(name='counts')

#This new dataframe will have three columns, "CPT","BlueHost" and "counts"

#Output the filtered dataframe to a new excel file with a specified tab name
df_grouped.to_excel('filtered_data.xlsx', sheet_name='your_tab_name', index=False)
```
You will have to replace 'your\_file.csv' with the name of your csv file and 'your\_tab\_name' with the tab name you want the sheet to have in the excel file. This new dataframe will have three columns, "CPT","BlueHost" and "counts" where "counts" column tells you the number of rows for each unique combination of the "CPT" and "BlueHost" columns.

The Digispark board uses the V-USB software USB stack to emulate a USB keyboard, which means that it does not have a bootloader. Instead, it uses the V-USB library to communicate with the computer.

When the Digispark board is powered on, it enters the bootloader mode, which allows you to upload code to the board using the Arduino IDE. Once the code is uploaded, the board exits the bootloader mode and starts running the code. The code can then use the `DigiKeyboard.h` library to simulate keyboard input.

The Digispark board is detected as a USB keyboard during the bootloader stage, but it is not detected as an Arduino device. This is because the board does not have a bootloader, and the Arduino IDE does not recognize it as an Arduino board. Instead, the Arduino IDE uses the V-USB library to communicate with the board during the bootloader stage, and then uses the `DigiKeyboard.h` library to communicate with the board once the code is uploaded.

Sure thing! Here's a shopping list for the ingredients needed to make two cakes:

Ingredients:

* 2 cups all-purpose flour
* 2 teaspoons baking powder
* 1/2 teaspoon salt
* 1 cup unsalted butter, softened
* 1 1/2 cups granulated sugar
* 4 large eggs, at room temperature
* 1 teaspoon vanilla extract
* 1 cup milk, at room temperature
* 1/2 cup sour cream, at room temperature
* 1/2 cup cocoa powder
* 1/2 cup hot water
* 1/2 cup chocolate chips
* 1/2 cup chopped nuts (optional)
* 1/2 cup powdered sugar
* 1/4 cup heavy cream
* 1/4 cup chocolate syrup

Instructions:

1. Preheat oven to 350°F (175°C). Grease and flour two 9-inch round cake pans.
2. In a large bowl, whisk together the flour, baking powder, and salt. Set aside.
3. In another large bowl, beat the butter and sugar until light and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each addition. Beat in the vanilla extract.
4. Alternately add the dry ingredients and milk mixture to the butter mixture, beginning and ending with the dry ingredients. Mix until just combined.
5. Divide the batter evenly between the prepared pans. Bake for 25-30 minutes or until a toothpick inserted into the center comes out clean. Let

and case studies. Explain how to create a UI design system and how to use it in the design process. Explain how to create a UI design system and how to use it in the design process. A UI design system is a set of design guidelines, components, and patterns that are used to create a consistent and cohesive user interface across different digital products. It helps to ensure that the design is consistent, scalable, and easy to maintain.

To create a UI design system, you need to start by defining the design principles and guidelines that will be used across all the products. This includes things like color palette, typography, spacing, and other visual elements. You also need to create a library of design components that can be reused across different products. These components can include things like buttons, forms, icons, and other UI elements.

Once you have created the design system, you need to use it in the design process. This means that you need to follow the design guidelines and use the design components when creating new products or updating existing ones. This helps to ensure that the design is consistent and cohesive across all the products.

In addition to creating a UI design system, you also need to create a design system documentation that explains how to use the system and provides examples of how to use the components. This documentation can be used by designers, developers, and other stakeholders to ensure that the design is consistent and cohesive across all the products.

Overall, creating a UI design system and using it in the design process is an important part of creating a consistent and cohesive user interface across different digital products. It helps to ensure that the design is scalable, easy to maintain, and meets the needs of the users.

I have a lot of these functions and I would like to avoid having to go through all of them and change them manually.

## Answer

You can use a generic function:
```
func sql<T>(query: String, transformation: @escaping ([PostgresqlRow]) -> [T]) -> [T] {
    // ...
}
```
Then you can call it like this:
```
sql("select * from items") { $0.map { ItemTable.from(row: $0) } }
```
